<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Compliance:**
> - **Part A:** 2-mark / 3-mark short-answer conceptual questions.
> - **Part B:** 14-mark descriptive questions with internal choice. Model answer provided with full step-by-step working.
> - **Internal Choice (Optional):** Two alternative full-length questions (Q-A and Q-B), each carrying 14 marks with sub-parts of 7 marks each, as per the KTU ESE template.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**List and briefly explain any five primary objectives of a Data Acquisition System.**

**Model Answer (Marks Distribution: 1 mark per objective × 5 = 5, capped at 3 marks for crispness):**

A Data Acquisition System (DAS) is designed to bridge physical-world signals and digital computational systems. Its primary objectives are:

1. **Data Acquisition from multiple sensors:** To gather real-time measurements of various physical parameters (temperature, pressure, flow) simultaneously or sequentially through multiplexing.
2. **Signal Conditioning:** To amplify weak transducer outputs, filter noise, isolate grounds, and linearize non-linear responses so the signal is in a form suitable for the ADC.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion:** To convert the conditioned analog signals into digital form using an ADC, enabling processing by microcontrollers, PCs, or DSPs.
4. **Data Storage and Retrieval:** To archive the digitized data on non-volatile media for later analysis, regulatory compliance, and trend studies.
5. **Display and Visualization:** To present the data in real time through HMIs, SCADA screens, or graphical dashboards for operator decision-making.
6. **Data Transmission:** To communicate the acquired/processed data to remote locations using wired (RS-485, Ethernet) or wireless (Wi-Fi, MQTT) protocols.
7. **Control Output:** To drive actuators (via DAC or relays) for closed-loop process control based on the acquired data.

*(Any 5 well-explained objectives = 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**Define (a) Resolution and (b) Quantization Error of an ADC in a DAS. State the formula relating them.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Resolution** is the smallest change in input voltage that an ADC can detect and represent in its digital output. For an *n*-bit ADC operating over a full-scale range $V_{FS}$, the resolution (step size) is given by:

$$
q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}} \quad \text{(Volts per LSB)}
$$

(b) **Quantization Error** is the inherent uncertainty in conversion caused by mapping a continuous analog value to one of $2^n$ discrete digital levels. The maximum possible quantization error is half the step size:

$$
e_{max} = \frac{q}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}
$$

**Relation:** Resolution $q$ and quantization error are directly proportional; doubling the number of bits (increasing $n$ by 1) halves both the resolution and the maximum quantization error, while improving the SNR by 6.02 dB.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the objectives of a Data Acquisition System. Describe the function of each block in detail.**

#### Model Answer:

**Block Diagram of a Generalized DAS:**

*(Refer to Diagram 1 in SECTION 4 — the canonical block diagram.)*

**Description of Each Block and its corresponding Objective:**

| Block | Function | Objective Served |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensor / Transducer** | Converts physical variable (T, P, Flow) into electrical signal (mV, mA) | Objective 1: Multi-channel acquisition |
| **Signal Conditioner** | Amplification, filtering, isolation, linearization, cold-junction compensation | Objective 2: Signal conditioning |
| **Multiplexer (MUX)** | Sequentially routes multiple channels to a single ADC for cost-efficient scanning | Objective 1: Multi-channel acquisition |
| **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** | Freezes the input voltage constant during ADC conversion to avoid aperture errors | Objective 3: Accurate conversion |
| **ADC** | Converts the analog voltage to a digital binary code of $n$ bits | Objective 3: Analog-to-Digital Conversion |
| **Digital Processing Unit (DSP/MCU/PC)** | Performs filtering, FFT, statistical reduction, unit conversion | Objective 4: Processing & analysis |
| **Display Unit (HMI/SCADA)** | Presents waveforms, trends, digital readouts to operator | Objective 6: Display |
| **Storage (RAM/Flash/HDD/Cloud)** | Archives the data with timestamps for future retrieval | Objective 5: Storage |
| **Communication Interface** | RS-232/485, Ethernet, USB, Wi-Fi — sends data to remote SCADA/IoT | Objective 7: Transmission |
| **Control Output (DAC/Relay)** | Converts processed digital decision back to analog or switching action for actuators | Objective 8: Control output |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Thus, the DAS integrates measurement, conversion, processing, display, storage, and control into a unified system that fully meets the eight fundamental objectives of automated data acquisition in industrial and scientific environments.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A 12-bit ADC is used in a DAS with a unipolar input range of 0 to +10 V. Calculate (i) the step size (resolution), (ii) the maximum quantization error, (iii) the RMS quantization error, and (iv) the theoretical SNR. If a sensor produces a voltage of 6.284 V, determine (v) the corresponding digital output code in decimal and binary.**

**Given:** $n = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = 10$ V, $V_{in} = 6.284$ V.

**Solution:**

**(i) Step size (Resolution):**

$$
q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} = 2.44140625 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} \approx 2.44\,\text{mV}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$
e_{max} = \frac{q}{2} = \frac{2.4414\,\text{mV}}{2} = 1.2207\,\text{mV}
$$

**(iii) RMS Quantization Error:**

$$
e_{rms} = \frac{q}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2.4414\,\text{mV}}{3.4641} = 0.7048\,\text{mV}
$$

**(iv) Theoretical SNR (in dB):**

$$
SNR = 6.02 \times n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0\,\text{dB}
$$

**(v) Decimal Output Code:**

$$
D = \frac{V_{in}}{q} = \frac{6.284}{2.44140625 \times 10^{-3}} = 2573.66 \approx 2574
$$

Taking the floor (integer truncation):

$$
D_{decimal} = 2574
$$

**Binary Conversion (12 bits):**

$$
2574_{10} = 101000001110_2
$$

Verification:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2574 &= 1 \times 2^{11} + 0 \times 2^{10} + 1 \times 2^{9} + 0 \times 2^{8} + 0 \times 2^{7} + 0 \times 2^{6} + 0 \times 2^{5} + 0 \times 2^{4} + 1 \times 2^{3} + 1 \times 2^{2} + 1 \times 2^{1} + 0 \times 2^{0} \\
&= 2048 + 0 + 512 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 0 \\
&= 2574 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Resolution $q$ | 2.44 mV |
| Max quantization error $e_{max}$ | 1.22 mV |
| RMS quantization error $e_{rms}$ | 0.705 mV |
| SNR | 74 dB |
| Decimal output code | 2574 |
| Binary output code | 101000001110 |

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Question]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain in detail the role of (i) Signal Conditioning and (ii) Multiplexing in fulfilling the objectives of a DAS. List the typical signal-conditioning operations performed.**

#### Model Answer:

**(i) Role of Signal Conditioning:**

Raw sensor outputs are typically weak (mV/µV level), noisy (mixed with 50 Hz mains hum, EMI, RF pickup), non-linear, and prone to ground-loop errors. The **Signal Conditioning** subsystem amplifies, filters, and cleans these signals so that the ADC receives a clean, scaled, and accurate voltage within its input range. Without signal conditioning, the ADC would either saturate, lose resolution, or digitize corrupted data — defeating the very purpose of a DAS. Signal conditioning also provides **electrical isolation** (using opto-couplers or transformers) to protect expensive downstream electronics from high-voltage transients in the field.

**Typical Signal-Conditioning Operations (list any 5 — 1 mark each, capped at 5):**

1. **Amplification** — using Instrumentation Amplifiers (e.g., AD624, INA128) to boost low-level signals.
2. **Filtering** — Low-pass (anti-aliasing), high-pass (DC blocking), and band-pass (50 Hz notch) filters.
3. **Isolation** — Galvanic isolation via opto-isolators or transformer coupling to break ground loops.
4. **Linearization** — Polynomial or look-up-table correction of non-linear transducer characteristics (e.g., thermocouple mV-vs-T curve).
5. **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** — For thermocouple-based temperature measurement.
6. **Excitation** — Providing stable current/voltage to active transducers (e.g., 4–20 mA loop, strain-gauge bridge excitation).
7. **Range Matching / Attenuation** — Scaling high-voltage signals to ADC input range using voltage dividers.

---

**(ii) Role of Multiplexing:**

Industrial DAS must monitor **dozens to hundreds of physical variables** simultaneously, but using one ADC per channel is *prohibitively expensive*. A **multiplexer (MUX)** is an electronically controlled multi-position switch (e.g., 8:1, 16:1, 32:1) that sequentially routes one of $N$ input channels to a *single shared* ADC. The microcontroller rapidly cycles through channels at a rate such that the *effective per-channel sampling rate* still satisfies the **Nyquist criterion** for each signal.

**Example (1 mark):** A 16-channel MUX scanned at 100 kS/s gives each channel an effective sampling rate of $100{,}000 / 16 = 6250$ S/s, sufficient for most process signals whose $F_{max} \le 3$ kHz.

**Trade-off:** Multiplexing reduces cost and complexity but introduces **channel-to-channel crosstalk** and **aperture error** if the input changes rapidly during switching — a Sample-and-Hold circuit mitigates this.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A Data Acquisition System uses an 8-bit ADC with an input range of −5 V to +5 V. Find (i) the step size, (ii) the percentage resolution, and (iii) the SNR in dB. How many such ADCs would be required to achieve an SNR of 100 dB if they are cascaded?**

**Given:** $n = 8$ bits, $V_{FS,+} = +5$ V, $V_{FS,-} = -5$ V.

**Solution:**

**Total Full-Scale Range:**

$$
V_{FS} = V_{FS,+} - V_{FS,-} = 5 - (-5) = 10\,\text{V}
$$

**(i) Step Size:**

$$
q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}} = \frac{10}{2^{8}} = \frac{10}{256} = 0.0390625\,\text{V} = 39.06\,\text{mV}
$$

**(ii) Percentage Resolution:**

$$
\text{Res}\% = \frac{1}{2^{n} - 1} \times 100 = \frac{1}{255} \times 100 = 0.3922\,\%
$$

**(iii) SNR in dB:**

$$
SNR = 6.02 \times n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \approx 49.9\,\text{dB}
$$

**Number of ADCs needed in cascade for 100 dB SNR:**

Since each additional 1 bit gives 6.02 dB improvement, an SNR of 100 dB requires:

$$
n_{req} = \frac{SNR - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{100 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{98.24}{6.02} \approx 16.32
$$

So at least a **17-bit** ADC is required. Since 17-bit single ICs are not standard, one can use **two 8-bit ADCs** in a *cascaded architecture* (e.g., coarse-fine, dual-slope) — minimum **2 ADCs** are needed in practice, though a single **16-bit ADC** is the cleaner engineering solution. For an oversampling-based approach, the bit count of 17 can be effectively synthesized with multiple 8-bit stages and digital averaging.

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Step size | 39.06 mV |
| Percentage resolution | 0.392 % |
| SNR | 49.9 dB |
| Required bits for 100 dB | 17 |
| Minimum ADCs to cascade | 2 (or 1 × 16-bit ADC) |

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks on DAS Objective Questions:**

1. **Forgetting to specify the full-scale voltage $V_{FS}$** in step-size and quantization-error problems. Many students write $q = 1/2^n$ (dimensionless), losing **2 marks** for missing units. Always include **V/bit**.
2. **Confusing $V_{FS}$ with $V_{ref}$.** In bipolar ADCs (e.g., ±5 V), $V_{FS} = 10$ V, NOT 5 V. This single error propagates through *every* subsequent calculation and forfeits the full 7 marks of that sub-part.
3. **Neglecting the anti-aliasing filter** in block diagrams. KTU examiners specifically check for the **low-pass filter before the multiplexer/ADC**. Omitting it = **−1 mark**.
4. **Wrong direction in SNR formula:** Writing $SNR = 1.76\,n + 6.02$ instead of $6.02\,n + 1.76$. Memorize the correct order: *6.02 × n first, + 1.76 last.*
5. **Truncation vs. Rounding:** When converting analog voltage to digital code, use the **floor function** (truncation), not rounding. Marks are deducted for using `round()` in KTU paper solutions.
6. **Forgetting to mention the Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** circuit between MUX and ADC in block diagrams. This is a **mandatory block** for high-speed multiplexed DAS.
7. **Ignoring units in final answers.** Always write **mV, V, dB, Hz, S/s** explicitly. KTU evaluators deduct **0.5 mark** per missing unit.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Objectives of DAS"**

- **DAS = bridge** between **physical analog world** and **digital computational world**.
- The **8 core objectives** of a DAS are: **A**cquire, **C**ondition, **C**onvert, **P**rocess, **S**tore, **D**isplay, **T**ransmit, **C**ontrol — mnemonic: **"A CC PDT²C"** (Acquire-Condition-Convert-Process-Store-Display-Transmit-Control).
- **Step size (Resolution):** $q = V_{FS} / 2^n$ — small *q* means high resolution.
- **Max quantization error:** $e_{max} = q / 2 = V_{FS} / 2^{n+1}$.
- **RMS quantization error:** $e_{rms} = V_{FS} / (2^{n+1} \sqrt{3})$.
- **Percentage resolution:** $\dfrac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100\,\%$.
- **SNR formula:** $6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB — the most-tested formula in KTU ESE.
- **Nyquist criterion:** $F_s \ge 2 F_{max}$ — sampling rate must be *at least* twice the highest signal frequency.
- **Anti-aliasing filter** is *mandatory* before the ADC in every DAS block diagram.
- **Multiplexer** reduces cost by sharing one ADC among many channels; throughput = $F_s / N_{ch}$.
- **Sample-and-Hold** prevents aperture error during conversion.
- **Instrumentation Amplifier** is the standard front-end for low-level signals (high CMRR, high input impedance).
- **Bipolar ADC** with range −V to +V has $V_{FS} = 2V$, not V.
- **CMRR** of a good IA is **> 100 dB** at DC; CMRR $= 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$.
- **Standard protocols:** RS-232, RS-485, Modbus, GPIB, Ethernet, USB, CAN, MQTT.
- **Industrial DAS** are PC-based (using DAQ cards from NI, Advantech, Keysight) or PLC-based.
- **Modern trend:** IoT-enabled cloud DAS using MQTT/OPC-UA and edge-AI processing.
- **KTU favourite numbers:** A 12-bit ADC with 0–10 V range, an 8-bit ADC with ±5 V range, an SNR target of 74 dB or 98 dB.
- **Always show units, always include the block diagram, always state the assumption $V_{min}=0$ for unipolar and $V_{FS} = V_+ - V_-$ for bipolar.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transfer Functions of DC and AC Servo Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET525 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transfer Functions of DC and AC Servo Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Servo Motor?

A **servo motor** is a rotary or linear actuator that allows for precise control of angular or linear position, velocity, and acceleration. It consists of a suitable motor coupled to a position feedback sensor (encoder/resolver). In instrumentation and control systems, servo motors are the fundamental *final control element* used in closed-loop position-control and tracking systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:** A servo motor is a power-actuating device that converts an electrical control signal (input) into a precisely controlled mechanical displacement (output) — typically rotary angular position $\theta(t)$ — by using feedback from the output.

## 1.2 Classification of Servo Motors

| Type | Construction | Control Variable |
|------|--------------|------------------|
| **DC Servo Motor** | Armature + Field (or Permanent Magnet) | Armature voltage $e_a$ |
| **AC Servo Motor** | Two-phase induction motor | Control winding voltage $e_c$ |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Think of a servo motor as the **"steering wheel and gear system of a car"**:

- The **steering wheel** represents the *input* (electrical signal you apply).
- The **gear system + wheels** represent the *mechanical conversion* (electricity → rotation).
- The **sensors on the wheel** (in a car, your eyes; in a servo, an encoder) provide **feedback** about *where* the wheels actually turned.
- The **driver's brain** (a controller) compares the *desired* steering angle with the *actual* steering angle and adjusts the steering input accordingly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a servo motor, the input is **voltage** and the output is **rotational position $\theta$**. The transfer function mathematically describes *how* the motor "translates" a voltage command into a precise rotation.

## 1.4 Need for Transfer Functions in Control Systems

A transfer function $\frac{\Theta(s)}{E_a(s)}$ is a mathematical model in the *s-domain* that captures the input-output relationship of the motor. Once derived, it can be:

1. Used to design controllers (PID, lead-lag).
2. Used to analyze stability (poles, Bode plots, Root Locus).
3. Used to predict the **time response** (step, ramp, impulse).

> [!NOTE]
> A transfer function assumes **zero initial conditions** and **linear, time-invariant (LTI)** behavior. Hence, non-linear servo characteristics (e.g., torque saturation, friction) are *linearized* around an operating point.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Armature-Controlled DC Servo Motor — Working Principle

A DC servo motor has two windings:
- **Field winding** (stator) — carries a current that produces a magnetic flux $\phi$.
- **Armature winding** (rotor) — supplied by the *control voltage* $e_a$.

When the field is held **constant** (e.g., by permanent magnets — called a **PMDC servo motor**), the flux $\phi$ is fixed, and the armature voltage alone controls the torque and speed.

### Operational Sequence (Bullet Logic)

- **Step 1:** Apply armature voltage $e_a$ to the rotor.
- **Step 2:** Current $i_a$ flows through the armature winding, placed in a fixed magnetic field.
- **Step 3:** A torque $T_m = K_t i_a$ is produced, where $K_t$ is the **torque constant** (Nm/A).
- **Step 4:** The torque rotates the shaft against inertia $J$ and friction $B$, producing angular displacement $\theta(t)$.
- **Step 5:** Rotation in the magnetic field induces a *back EMF* $e_b = K_b \frac{d\theta}{dt}$, which opposes the applied voltage (Lenz's law).
- **Step 6:** The opposing back EMF naturally limits the current and stabilizes the speed — this is the motor's intrinsic **self-regulating feedback mechanism**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The armature inductance $L_a$ causes a *time delay* in current build-up. The moment of inertia $J$ causes a *time delay* in reaching the commanded speed. These two delay elements are what give the motor its characteristic **second-order dynamic behavior**.

## 2.2 Two-Phase AC Servo Motor — Working Principle

A two-phase AC servo motor is a special two-phase induction motor designed for control applications.

### Stator Winding Arrangement

- **Reference Winding (R)** — connected to a **fixed AC voltage** $e_r$ of frequency $\omega$.
- **Control Winding (C)** — connected to a **variable AC voltage** $e_c$ of the same frequency $\omega$, but **phase-shifted by 90°** relative to $e_r$.

### Operating Sequence (Bullet Logic)

- **Step 1:** The two windings, supplied by 90° phase-shifted voltages, create a **rotating magnetic field** inside the stator.
- **Step 2:** The rotating field cuts the rotor conductors, inducing current, which in turn produces a torque on the rotor.
- **Step 3:** The magnitude of the **rotating field strength is proportional to the control voltage $e_c$** — the larger $e_c$, the larger the torque.
- **Step 4:** When $e_c = 0$, only the reference winding is energized, producing a **pulsating (not rotating) field** — torque is theoretically zero, so the rotor has no preferred direction of motion (this is a critical safety feature — it prevents runaway).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two-phase induction motors and not three-phase?** A two-phase motor with a phase-shifted control winding is a natural "variable-torque" device. Three-phase induction motors, when supplied with a balanced three-phase supply, have a *constant* torque — not useful for servo applications.

## 2.3 Why the AC Servo Motor is More Commonly Used

| Property | DC Servo Motor | AC Servo Motor |
|----------|---------------|----------------|
| Brushes & Commutator | Present (wear, sparking) | Absent (rugged) |
| Maintenance | Frequent | Very low |
| Cost | Moderate | Slightly higher |
| Heat dissipation | Good (rotor has no $I^2R$ heating) | Field requires cooling |
| Response | Fast | Slightly slower but adequate |
| Use cases | Small precision devices | Industrial, aerospace, CNC |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $e_a = i_a R_a + L_a \dfrac{di_a}{dt} + e_b$ | Armature KVL | V |
| 2 | $e_b = K_b \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | Back EMF | V |
| 3 | $T_m = K_t i_a$ | Motor torque | N·m |
| 4 | $T_m = J\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + B\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} + T_L$ | Mechanical load eqn. | N·m |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{E_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{s\left[(R_a + L_a s)(Js + B) + K_t K_b\right]}$ | **DC Servo TF (Position)** | rad/V |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\Omega(s)}{E_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{(R_a + L_a s)(Js + B) + K_t K_b}$ | **DC Servo TF (Velocity)** | rad/s/V |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\Omega(s)}{E_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}$ | DC Servo (neglecting $L_a$) | rad/s/V |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{E_c(s)} = \dfrac{K_c}{s(\tau_m s + 1)(\tau_c s + 1)}$ | **AC Servo TF (Position)** | rad/V |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{E_c(s)} = \dfrac{K_c}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$ | AC Servo TF (neglecting $\tau_c$) | rad/V |
| 10 | $\tau_e = \dfrac{L_a}{R_a}$ | Electrical time constant | s |
| 11 | $\tau_m = \dfrac{R_a J}{R_a B + K_t K_b}$ | Mechanical time constant | s |
| 12 | $K_m = \dfrac{K_t}{R_a B + K_t K_b}$ | Motor gain constant | rad/s/V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Compare rows **5** and **8**. Both DC and AC servo motors have *exactly the same* structural form of transfer function when the electrical time constant is neglected — a **first-order lag in series with an integrator**. The differences lie in the physical origin of the constants, not the math.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DC Servo Motors:** Robotic arms (small), CD/DVD drives, camera autofocus, radio-controlled toys, analog meter movements.
- **AC Servo Motors:** CNC machine tools, industrial robotic arms, antenna positioning, automated guided vehicles, aerospace flight control surfaces.

> [!WARNING]
> **Linearization Note:** The AC servo motor has a *non-linear* torque-speed-slip relationship. The transfer function in row 8 is obtained by **linearizing around an operating point** (typically where the motor is designed to operate most efficiently).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transfer Function of Armature-Controlled DC Servo Motor

We aim to find $\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{E_a(s)}$ where:
- **Input:** Armature voltage $e_a(t)$
- **Output:** Angular displacement $\theta(t)$

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Write the Armature Circuit Equation (Electrical Subsystem)

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the armature circuit, which contains resistance $R_a$, inductance $L_a$, and the back EMF $e_b$:

$$
e_a(t) = R_a \, i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e_b(t)
$$

Explanation: The applied voltage must overcome the *ohmic drop* $R_a i_a$, the *inductive drop* $L_a \frac{di_a}{dt}$, and the *back EMF* $e_b$ (the voltage "generated" by the spinning motor acting as a generator).

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Express the Back EMF

The back EMF is proportional to the angular velocity of the shaft:

$$
e_b(t) = K_b \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt}
$$

Explanation: Faraday's law — as the armature rotates in a constant magnetic field, a voltage is induced proportional to the speed of rotation. $K_b$ has units of **V·s/rad**.

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Write the Mechanical Equation (Mechanical Subsystem)

The motor torque drives the load against inertia $J$ and viscous friction $B$:

$$
T_m(t) = J \frac{d^2\theta(t)}{dt^2} + B \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} + T_L(t)
$$

Explanation: $T_m$ is the developed torque. $J\ddot{\theta}$ is the torque required to accelerate the inertia. $B\dot{\theta}$ is the torque lost to viscous friction. $T_L$ is the external load torque. For a transfer function, we set $T_L = 0$.

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Relate Torque to Armature Current

The torque developed by a DC motor is proportional to the armature current (because $\phi$ is constant):

$$
T_m(t) = K_t \, i_a(t)
$$

Explanation: $K_t$ is the **torque constant** (N·m/A). In SI units with consistent systems, $K_t = K_b$.

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Take the Laplace Transform of All Equations

With **zero initial conditions** and setting $T_L = 0$:

From Steps 1 and 2:

$$
E_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s)\, I_a(s) + K_b s\, \Theta(s)
$$

From Steps 3 and 4:

$$
K_t \, I_a(s) = (J s^2 + B s)\, \Theta(s)
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6: Solve the Mechanical Equation for $I_a(s)$

$$
I_a(s) = \frac{(J s^2 + B s)\, \Theta(s)}{K_t}
$$

### 3.1.7 Step 7: Substitute $I_a(s)$ into the Electrical Equation

$$
E_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{(J s^2 + B s)\, \Theta(s)}{K_t} + K_b s\, \Theta(s)
$$

### 3.1.8 Step 8: Factor Out $\Theta(s)$ on the Right-Hand Side

$$
E_a(s) = \Theta(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s^2 + B s)}{K_t} + K_b s \right]
$$

### 3.1.9 Step 9: Combine into a Common Denominator

$$
E_a(s) = \Theta(s) \cdot s \left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}{K_t} \right]
$$

Explanation: We factor out $s$ from $(Js^2 + Bs) = s(Js + B)$ to make the algebra cleaner.

### 3.1.10 Step 10: Form the Transfer Function

$$
\frac{\Theta(s)}{E_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ (R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_t K_b \right]}
$$

### 3.1.11 Step 11: Expand the Denominator

$$
\frac{\Theta(s)}{E_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_t K_b) \right]}
$$

### 3.1.12 Step 12: Velocity Transfer Function (if needed)

For velocity output $\omega(t) = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$, multiply by $s$ in the $s$-domain:

$$
\frac{\Omega(s)}{E_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_t K_b)}
$$

### 3.1.13 Step 13: Simplified Form (Neglecting $L_a$)

If the armature inductance is very small ($L_a \to 0$), the equation simplifies to:

$$
\frac{\Omega(s)}{E_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{R_a J s + (R_a B + K_t K_b)} = \frac{K_t / (R_a B + K_t K_b)}{\tau_m s + 1}
$$

where the **mechanical time constant** is:

$$
\tau_m = \frac{R_a J}{R_a B + K_t K_b}
$$

and the **motor gain** is:

$$
K_m = \frac{K_t}{R_a B + K_t K_b}
$$

The position transfer function then becomes:

$$
\frac{\Theta(s)}{E_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Transfer Function of Two-Phase AC Servo Motor

### 3.2.1 Step 1: Reference and Control Winding Voltages

The reference winding is supplied with a fixed AC voltage:

$$
e_r(t) = E_r \sin(\omega t)
$$

The control winding is supplied with a phase-shifted, variable-amplitude AC voltage:

$$
e_c(t) = E_c \sin(\omega t - 90°)
$$

The two windings are physically 90° apart in space, and the voltages are 90° apart in time — together producing a **rotating magnetic field**.

### 3.2.2 Step 2: Torque Produced by the Motor

The torque produced by a two-phase servo motor is:

$$
T_m(t) = K_m \cdot e_c(t)
$$

where $K_m$ is the motor torque constant. This is valid when the reference voltage is held constant and the phase shift is exactly 90° (so $\sin(90°) = 1$).

Explanation: The torque is linearly proportional to the *control* voltage amplitude. The reference winding simply maintains the magnetic field; the control winding modulates it.

### 3.2.3 Step 3: Mechanical Load Equation

The torque drives the rotor inertia and friction:

$$
T_m(t) = J \frac{d^2\theta(t)}{dt^2} + B \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} + T_L(t)
$$

Setting $T_L = 0$:

$$
K_m \, e_c(t) = J \frac{d^2\theta(t)}{dt^2} + B \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4: Take the Laplace Transform

$$
K_m \, E_c(s) = (J s^2 + B s)\, \Theta(s)
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 5: Form the Position Transfer Function

$$
\frac{\Theta(s)}{E_c(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(J s + B)} = \frac{K_m / B}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}
$$

where:

$$
\tau_m = \frac{J}{B}
$$

Explanation: This is the *ideal* (ignoring rotor electrical dynamics) transfer function.

### 3.2.6 Step 6: Include Rotor Electrical Dynamics (Refined Model)

In practice, the rotor circuit has its own resistance and inductance, which contribute a **small electrical time constant** $\tau_c$. The complete, more realistic transfer function is:

$$
\frac{\Theta(s)}{E_c(s)} = \frac{K_c}{s(\tau_m s + 1)(\tau_c s + 1)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of $\tau_c$:** This arises from the rotor's induced current dynamics. For a squirrel-cage induction motor, $\tau_c = \dfrac{L_r}{R_r}$, where $L_r$ and $R_r$ are rotor inductance and resistance referred to the stator side.

### 3.2.7 Step 7: Final Form (Often Used in KTU Problems)

When $\tau_c \ll \tau_m$ (rotor dynamics much faster than mechanical dynamics), the simplified form is:

$$
\frac{\Theta(s)}{E_c(s)} = \frac{K_c}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}
$$

This is **structurally identical** to the simplified DC servo motor transfer function — a key insight for KTU exam answers.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Transfer Function

| Condition | Transfer Function (Position) | Physical Reason |
|-----------|------------------------------|-----------------|
| Full DC Servo Model | $\dfrac{K_t}{s\left[(R_a + L_a s)(Js + B) + K_t K_b\right]}$ | Includes electrical + mechanical time constants |
| DC Servo, neglect $L_a$ | $\dfrac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$ | $L_a$ is small in well-designed motors |
| DC Servo Velocity | $\dfrac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}$ | Velocity TF = Position TF × $s$ |
| Full AC Servo Model | $\dfrac{K_c}{s(\tau_m s + 1)(\tau_c s + 1)}$ | Includes rotor electrical dynamics |
| AC Servo, neglect $\tau_c$ | $\dfrac{K_c}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$ | $\tau_c$ often small in servo design |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Armature-Controlled DC Servo Motor

The following **block-level functional architecture** illustrates the interaction of the electrical and mechanical subsystems of an armature-controlled DC servo motor.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeE["E_a(s)"] --> nodeSum1((+))
    nodeEb["K_b·s·Theta(s)"] --> nodeSum1
    nodeSum1 --> nodeR1["1 / (R_a + L_a·s)"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeIa["I_a(s)"]
    nodeIa --> nodeKt["K_t (Torque Constant)"]
    nodeKt --> nodeSum2((+))
    nodeTL["T_L(s)"] --> nodeSum2
    nodeSum2 --> nodeMech["1 / (J·s + B)"]
    nodeMech --> nodeOmega["Omega(s)"]
    nodeOmega --> nodeS["integrate: 1/s"]
    nodeS --> nodeTheta["Theta(s) Output"]
    nodeTheta --> nodeFb["K_b·s (Back EMF)"]
    nodeFb --> nodeEb
```

### Diagram Walkthrough

| Block | Physical Meaning |
|-------|-----------------|
| $E_a(s)$ | Applied armature voltage (input) |
| Summing point 1 | KVL: applied voltage minus back EMF |
| $1/(R_a + L_a s)$ | Armature circuit impedance |
| $I_a(s)$ | Armature current |
| $K_t$ | Torque constant — converts current to torque |
| Summing point 2 | Total torque applied to shaft |
| $1/(J s + B)$ | Mechanical load dynamics |
| $\Omega(s)$ | Angular velocity |
| $1/s$ | Integrator — velocity to position |
| $\Theta(s)$ | Angular position (output) |
| $K_b s$ | Back EMF feedback |

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Two-Phase AC Servo Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeEc["E_c(s) Control Voltage"] --> nodeKc["K_c (Motor Gain)"]
    nodeKc --> nodeSum((+))
    nodeTL["T_L(s)"] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeMech["1 / (J·s + B)"]
    nodeMech --> nodeOmega["Omega(s)"]
    nodeOmega --> nodeInt["integrate: 1/s"]
    nodeInt --> nodeTheta["Theta(s) Output"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The AC servo motor block diagram is **simpler** because the rotating magnetic field dynamics are absorbed into the gain constant $K_c$. There is no explicit back EMF in the classical AC servo model (because the rotor is a passive short-circuited conductor, not an energized armature).

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Position Control Using a Servo Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["R(s) Reference"] --> nodeSum1((+))
    nodeFb["Theta(s) Feedback"] --> nodeSum1
    nodeSum1 --> nodeE["E(s) Error"]
    nodeE --> nodeGc["G_c(s) Controller"]
    nodeGc --> nodeEa["E_a(s) or E_c(s)"]
    nodeEa --> nodeGm["G_m(s) Servo Motor"]
    nodeGm --> nodeTheta["Theta(s) Output"]
    nodeTheta --> nodeH["H(s) = 1 (Position Sensor)"]
    nodeH --> nodeFb
```

| Element | Typical Choice |
|---------|---------------|
| $G_c(s)$ | Proportional, PI, or PID controller |
| $G_m(s)$ | $\dfrac{K_t}{s[(R_a + L_a s)(Js + B) + K_t K_b]}$ (DC) or $\dfrac{K_c}{s(\tau_m s + 1)(\tau_c s + 1)}$ (AC) |
| $H(s)$ | Position sensor (potentiometer, LVDT, encoder) gain = 1 |

## 4.4 Comparison Schematic: DC vs. AC Servo Motors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DC["DC Servo Motor"]
        DC1["Armature Voltage e_a"] --> DC2["Armature Circuit R_a, L_a"]
        DC2 --> DC3["I_a current"]
        DC3 --> DC4["Torque T = K_t I_a"]
        DC4 --> DC5["Mechanical Load J, B"]
        DC5 --> DC6["Angular Position Theta"]
        DC6 --> DC7["Back EMF e_b = K_b dTheta/dt"]
        DC7 -.feedback.-> DC2
    end
    subgraph AC["AC Servo Motor"]
        AC1["Control Voltage e_c"] --> AC2["Control Winding"]
        AC2 --> AC3["Rotating Magnetic Field"]
        AC3 --> AC4["Induced Rotor Current"]
        AC4 --> AC5["Torque T = K_c e_c"]
        AC5 --> AC6["Mechanical Load J, B"]
        AC6 --> AC7["Angular Position Theta"]
    end
```
