# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q:** Define the following terms with respect to frequency domain stability analysis:
(a) Gain Margin
(b) Phase Margin

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Gain Margin (GM):** Gain Margin is defined as the factor by which the open-loop gain $K$ can be **multiplied** before the system reaches the verge of instability. Mathematically, it is the reciprocal of the magnitude of the open-loop transfer function evaluated at the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$.

$$GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$$

In decibels: $GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM)$.

**(b) Phase Margin (PM):** Phase Margin is defined as the additional amount of phase lag (in degrees) that can be added to the open-loop transfer function at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ before the system becomes unstable.

$$PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

A system is stable if both GM > 0 dB and PM > 0°.

### Question A2: Short Application
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Q:** For a system with open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$, the phase crossover frequency is $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5}$ rad/s. Calculate the Gain Margin.

**Model Answer:**

Given $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5}$ rad/s.

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \dfrac{10}{\omega_{pc}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^2 + 1}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^2 + 25}}$$

$$= \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{5}\cdot\sqrt{5+1}\cdot\sqrt{5+25}} = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{5}\cdot\sqrt{6}\cdot\sqrt{30}}$$

$$= \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{5 \cdot 6 \cdot 30}} = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{900}} = \dfrac{10}{30} = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

$$GM = \dfrac{1}{1/3} = 3 \quad \text{(linear)}$$

$$GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(3) = 9.54 \text{ dB}$$

Since $GM_{dB} > 0$, the system is **stable**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts typically of **7 marks + 7 marks**. Two alternative questions are provided below (Answer ANY ONE).

### Question B-A: Comprehensive Nyquist Analysis (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q:** For a unity feedback control system with open-loop transfer function:
$$G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the Nyquist plot for $K = 12$ and determine the stability of the closed-loop system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable, and compute the gain margin and phase margin for $K = 6$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Nyquist Plot Sketch & Stability for $K = 12$:**

**Step 1:** Substitute $s = j\omega$:
$$G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K}{j\omega(j\omega+2)(j\omega+4)}$$

**Step 2:** Expand the denominator:
$$(j\omega+2)(j\omega+4) = j\omega\cdot j\omega + j\omega\cdot 4 + 2\cdot j\omega + 2\cdot 4$$
$$= -\omega^2 + 6j\omega + 8 = (8-\omega^2) + j6\omega$$

So the full denominator is:
$$j\omega[(8-\omega^2) + j6\omega] = j\omega(8-\omega^2) + j^2\cdot 6\omega^2 = -6\omega^2 + j\omega(8-\omega^2)$$

Therefore:
$$G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K}{-6\omega^2 + j\omega(8-\omega^2)}$$

**Step 3:** Magnitude and Phase:
$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{K}{\omega\sqrt{(8-\omega^2)^2 + 36\omega^2}}$$
$$\angle G(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\omega(8-\omega^2)}{-6\omega^2}\right) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{8-\omega^2}{-6\omega}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Identify Key Frequencies:
- At $\omega = 0^+$: $\vert G \vert \to \infty$, phase $\to -90°$ (since one pole at origin).
- At $\omega = \sqrt{8}$: denominator real part = 0, phase = $-180°$ (phase crossover).
- At $\omega \to \infty$: $\vert G \vert \to 0$, phase $\to -270°$ (3 poles contribute $-270°$).

**Step 5:** Compute $\omega_{pc}$ for $K = 12$:
$$\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6:** Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$:
$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \dfrac{12}{2\sqrt{2}\cdot\sqrt{0 + 36\cdot 8}} = \dfrac{12}{2\sqrt{2}\cdot\sqrt{288}} = \dfrac{12}{2\sqrt{2}\cdot 12\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{12}{48} = 0.25$$

**Step 7:** Since the plot **does not encircle** $-1+j0$ (crosses negative real axis at $-0.25$, which is to the right of $-1$), and $P = 0$ (no open-loop RHP poles), $Z = N + P = 0 + 0 = 0$. **System is stable for $K = 12$.**

---

**Part (b) — Range of K and Margins for K = 6:**

**Step 1:** Critical condition: $\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = 1$
$$\dfrac{K}{2\sqrt{2}\cdot\sqrt{288}} = 1 \implies K = 48$$

**Step 2:** For stability, the crossing on the negative real axis must be **between $-1$ and $0$** (i.e., magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ must be less than 1, meaning $K < 48$).

**Step 3:** Range of $K$ for stability: $0 < K < 48$.

**Step 4:** For $K = 6$, compute GM:
$$GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert} = \dfrac{1}{6/48} = 8 \text{ (linear)}$$
$$GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(8) = 18.06 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5:** For PM, find $\omega_{gc}$ where $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$:
$$\dfrac{6}{\omega_{gc}\sqrt{(8-\omega_{gc}^2)^2 + 36\omega_{gc}^2}} = 1$$

For low frequencies (before resonance effects), approximate $\omega_{gc}$ from:
$$\dfrac{6}{\omega_{gc}\cdot 8} \approx 1 \implies \omega_{gc} \approx 0.75 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6:** Phase at $\omega_{gc} \approx 0.75$:
$$\angle G(j0.75) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{8-0.5625}{-6\cdot 0.75}\right) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{7.4375}{-4.5}\right) \approx -121.2°$$

Wait, careful with quadrant:
The real part is negative ($\omega < \sqrt{8}$), imaginary part is negative ($8-\omega^2 > 0$, so numerator is positive, but the negative sign in front places it in third quadrant, so phase is in $[-180°, -90°]$).

$$\angle G(j0.75) \approx -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.75/2) - \tan^{-1}(0.75/4) = -90° - 20.56° - 10.62° = -121.18°$$

**Step 7:** Phase Margin:
$$PM = 180° + (-121.18°) = 58.82°$$

This is a **healthy phase margin**, indicating good relative stability.

---

### Question B-B: Bode Plot Stability Analysis (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Q:** A unity feedback system has open-loop transfer function:
$$G(s) = \dfrac{20(1+0.5s)}{s(1+5s)(1+0.1s)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the Bode plot (magnitude and phase) and find the gain crossover frequency and phase crossover frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the Gain Margin and Phase Margin. Comment on the stability of the system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Bode Plot Sketch:**

**Step 1:** Identify corner frequencies:
- Pole at origin: contributes $-20$ dB/decade from $\omega = 0$.
- Zero at $\omega = 1/0.5 = 2$ rad/s: slope change $+20$ dB/decade.
- Pole at $\omega = 1/5 = 0.2$ rad/s: slope change $-20$ dB/decade.
- Pole at $\omega = 1/0.1 = 10$ rad/s: slope change $-20$ dB/decade.

**Step 2:** Magnitude Plot Slope Summary:
- $\omega < 0.2$: slope = $-20$ dB/decade.
- $0.2 < \omega < 2$: slope = $-40$ dB/decade.
- $2 < \omega < 10$: slope = $-20$ dB/decade.
- $\omega > 10$: slope = $-40$ dB/decade.

**Step 3:** Initial magnitude (using $s \to 0$ asymptote): $20\log_{10}(20) = 26.02$ dB.

**Step 4:** Reference magnitudes at corner frequencies:
- At $\omega = 0.2$: $26.02 - 20\log_{10}(0.2) = 26.02 + 13.98 = 40$ dB (using initial slope of $-20$).

Wait, recompute. At $\omega = 0.2$, magnitude is:
$$20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{20}{0.2}\right) = 20\log_{10}(100) = 40 \text{ dB}$$

- At $\omega = 2$: $40 - 40\log_{10}(2/0.2) = 40 - 40 = 0$ dB.

**Step 5:** So $\omega_{gc} \approx 2$ rad/s (precisely, slightly less due to corner frequency transitions).

**Step 6:** Phase Plot:
- Pole at origin: $-90°$ (constant).
- Pole at $\omega = 0.2$: $-90°$ transition centered at $\omega = 0.2$ (one decade below to one decade above).
- Zero at $\omega = 2$: $+90°$ transition centered at $\omega = 2$.
- Pole at $\omega = 10$: $-90°$ transition centered at $\omega = 10$.

**Step 7:** Phase crossover $\omega_{pc}$:
At $\omega = 0.2$: phase = $-90° - 45° = -135°$ (pole starting to act).
At $\omega = 2$: phase = $-90° - 90° + 45° = -135°$ (pole complete, zero starting).
At $\omega = 10$: phase = $-90° - 90° + 90° - 45° = -135°$.

Phase never reaches $-180°$ since each pole contributes max $-90°$ and the zero contributes $+90°$, with pole at origin at $-90°$.

**Conclusion:** $\omega_{pc}$ does not exist (no phase crossover). The phase is bounded between $-90°$ and approximately $-180° + 90° = -90°$ net... actually:
- Maximum negative phase: $-90°$ (origin) $- 90°$ (pole at 0.2) $- 90°$ (pole at 10) $+ 90°$ (zero at 2) = $-180°$.
- This is approached asymptotically but never exactly reached.

**Step 8:** More precisely, phase = $-180°$ requires:
$$-90° - \tan^{-1}(5\omega) + \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega) = -180°$$
$$\tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) - \tan^{-1}(5\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega) = -90°$$

As $\omega \to \infty$, $\tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) \to 90°$, $\tan^{-1}(5\omega) \to 90°$, $\tan^{-1}(0.1\omega) \to 90°$, so the LHS = $90° - 90° - 90° = -90°$, which is not $-90°$ in total, leaving phase at $-180°$ as the asymptotic value. **Phase crossover occurs only at $\omega \to \infty$.**

---

**Part (b) — Gain Margin and Phase Margin:**

**Step 1:** Since $\omega_{pc} = \infty$, the gain margin (at infinite frequency) becomes:
$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{\omega \to \infty} = \dfrac{20 \cdot 0.5\omega}{\omega \cdot 5\omega \cdot 0.1\omega} = \dfrac{10\omega}{0.5\omega^3} = \dfrac{20}{\omega^2} \to 0$$

**Step 2:** Gain Margin (linear):
$$GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\infty) \vert} = \dfrac{1}{0} \to \infty$$

**Step 3:** Gain Margin (dB):
$$GM_{dB} = \infty \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4:** Gain Crossover Frequency (precise):
Solve $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$:
$$\dfrac{20\sqrt{1+(0.5\omega_{gc})^2}}{\omega_{gc}\sqrt{1+(5\omega_{gc})^2}\sqrt{1+(0.1\omega_{gc})^2}} = 1$$

Approximating $\omega_{gc} \approx 2$ rad/s (from the Bode plot):
$$\dfrac{20\sqrt{1+1}}{2\sqrt{1+100}\sqrt{1+0.04}} = \dfrac{20\sqrt{2}}{2\sqrt{101}\sqrt{1.04}} \approx \dfrac{28.28}{20.5} \approx 1.38$$

So $\omega_{gc}$ is slightly less than 2. Solving iteratively, $\omega_{gc} \approx 1.65$ rad/s.

**Step 5:** Phase at $\omega_{gc} = 1.65$:
$$\phi = -90° - \tan^{-1}(5 \times 1.65) + \tan^{-1}(0.5 \times 1.65) - \tan^{-1}(0.1 \times 1.65)$$
$$= -90° - \tan^{-1}(8.25) + \tan^{-1}(0.825) - \tan^{-1}(0.165)$$
$$= -90° - 83.09° + 39.55° - 9.38°$$
$$= -142.92°$$

**Step 6:** Phase Margin:
$$PM = 180° + (-142.92°) = 37.08°$$

**Step 7:** Stability Comment:
- $GM = \infty$ dB (very high).
- $PM = 37.08°$ (acceptable, between 30° and 60°).
- **System is stable** with good gain margin but moderate phase margin.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in GM:** Students often forget the negative sign in $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$. If the plot crosses 0 dB **before** reaching $-180°$, the system is **unstable** and GM is **negative dB**.
> 2. **Confusing Gain and Phase Crossover:** $\omega_{gc}$ is where magnitude = 1, $\omega_{pc}$ is where phase = $-180°$. They are **different frequencies** in general.
> 3. **Miscounting Encirclements:** Always follow the **convention** (CCW positive, CW negative) consistently. Mirror the upper half of the Nyquist plot into the lower half (conjugate symmetry for real-coefficient systems).
> 4. **Indentation around poles on $j\omega$ axis:** If the open-loop system has poles on the imaginary axis (e.g., pure integrator), the Nyquist contour must detour around them with a small semicircle of radius $\epsilon \to 0$.
> 5. **Forgetting the +1 in $1 + G(s)H(s)$:** The critical point is $-1 + j0$, **not** the origin. A common mistake is to encircle-check the wrong point.
> 6. **Unit Confusion:** GM is dimensionless (linear) **and** in dB — they differ by $20\log_{10}$. Always state which one.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Stability in Frequency Domain: Quick-Fire Recall List**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **Nyquist Criterion** uses the open-loop $G(s)H(s)$ to predict closed-loop stability.
- ☐ **Stability Equation:** $Z = N + P$, where $Z$ = RHP closed-loop poles, $N$ = CCW encirclements of $-1+j0$, $P$ = open-loop RHP poles.
- ☐ **Absolute Stability:** $Z = 0$ (no closed-loop RHP poles).
- ☐ **Relative Stability:** quantified by **GM** and **PM**.

### Key Formulas
- ☐ $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ and $GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM)$.
- ☐ $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$.
- ☐ $M_r \approx \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ for second-order systems.
- ☐ $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$.

### Design Benchmarks
- ☐ **GM ≥ 6 dB** (minimum acceptable for industrial design).
- ☐ **PM = 30° to 60°** (good design range).
- ☐ **PM = 45°** often considered "optimal" for many servo systems.
- ☐ **Bandwidth** $\omega_B$ indicates speed of response — higher $\omega_B$ = faster response but typically lower PM.

### Plot Reading Rules
- ☐ On a **Nyquist plot**, count encirclements of $-1+j0$ point (CCW positive, CW negative).
- ☐ On a **Bode plot**, GM is read as the **dB value at $\omega_{pc}$** (negated), and PM is read as **$180° + \text{phase at } \omega_{gc}$**.
- ☐ If Bode plot crosses 0 dB with slope **$-20$ dB/decade** at $\omega_{gc}$ → stable.
- ☐ If slope at $\omega_{gc}$ is **$-40$ dB/decade** → marginally/conditionally stable.
- ☐ If slope at $\omega_{gc}$ is **$-60$ dB/decade or worse** → unstable.

### Common Pitfalls
- ☐ Don't confuse the **type** of the system (number of poles at origin) with **order** (total poles).
- ☐ Always check whether the system has open-loop RHP poles ($P$) before applying $Z = N + P$.
- ☐ For conditionally stable systems, GM and PM alone are **insufficient** — the complete Nyquist plot must be examined for additional encirclements.

### Connections to Other Modules
- ☐ **Time domain ↔ Frequency domain:** $PM \uparrow \Rightarrow \zeta \uparrow \Rightarrow$ less overshoot.
- ☐ **Routh-Hurwitz (Module 3) ↔ Nyquist:** Same stability conclusion, different methodology.
- ☐ **Root Locus (Module 3) ↔ Bode:** $j\omega$-axis crossing on root locus corresponds to phase crossover on Bode.

### Memory Aid
- ☐ **"GM is G's Margin"** — measured at the phase crossover (where G enters the danger zone of phase = $-180°$).
- ☐ **"PM is the Padding"** — measured at gain crossover, padding before the system loses phase coherence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gain Margin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET525 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gain Margin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gain Margin (GM)** is a frequency-domain relative stability measure that quantifies the factor by which the open-loop gain of a feedback control system can be uniformly increased before the closed-loop system reaches the verge of instability (i.e., the Nyquist contour just passes through the critical point −1 + j0).

Mathematically, the Gain Margin is evaluated at the **phase crossover frequency (ω_pc)**, which is the frequency at which the open-loop phase angle equals **−180°** (or an odd multiple of −180°). The formal expressions are:

$$
\text{GM} = \frac{1}{\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})\,H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert}
$$

$$
\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})\,H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert}\right) = -20 \log_{10}\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})\,H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert
$$

Where:
- $\omega_{pc}$ = Phase Crossover Frequency (rad/s)
- $G(s)H(s)$ = Open-Loop Transfer Function (OLTF)
- $j = \sqrt{-1}$ (imaginary unit)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:** Gain Margin is a mandatory topic under Module 4 (Frequency Response Analysis). It is a **relative stability index**, not an absolute one. A system can be stable with GM = 1.2, and a system with GM = 5 is *more* stable in a relative sense, though excessive GM leads to sluggish time-domain performance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Safety Brake" of a Control System

Imagine you are driving a car (the **closed-loop system**) along a winding mountain road with a guardrail on the cliff side. The guardrail represents the **−1 + j0 critical point**, and the trajectory of your car (the **Nyquist plot**) hugs the inside of the curve.

- **Phase Crossover Frequency (ω_pc):** The exact moment your steering wheel has turned by a critical angle that, if maintained, would send the car over the cliff.
- **Gain Margin:** The *extra distance* (or margin) between your current path and the cliff edge. It tells you, *"How much more can I press the accelerator (gain) before I lose control?"*

If the guardrail is far away (large GM → high dB value), the drive is safe. If the car is already scraping the guardrail (small GM), any small gain increase will cause a crash (instability).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Intuition:** A positive GM (in dB) means the system is stable. A negative GM means the Nyquist plot **encircles** the −1 + j0 point, indicating instability. GM = 0 dB is the *knife's edge* — the system is marginally stable and will exhibit sustained oscillations.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conventions

| Metric | Symbol | Value at Marginal Stability | Stability Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Gain Margin | $\text{GM}$ | 1 | $\text{GM} > 1$ |
| Logarithmic Gain Margin | $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}}$ | **0 dB** | $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} > 0 \text{ dB}$ |
| Phase Crossover Frequency | $\omega_{pc}$ | — | Must exist (real, positive) |

The physical constant of practical importance is the **unity gain reference (0 dB line)**, against which the magnitude curve is measured on a Bode plot.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Computation

The procedure to compute GM in a KTU examination follows a strict algorithmic flow:

1. **Obtain the Open-Loop Transfer Function (OLTF)** $G(s)H(s)$ from the block diagram of the system.
2. **Substitute $s = j\omega$** to convert the OLTF into the frequency response form $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$.
3. **Compute the phase angle** $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ as a function of $\omega$.
4. **Find the Phase Crossover Frequency (ω_pc):** Solve the equation $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180°$ (or $-180° \cdot n$ for odd integers $n$).
   - Equivalently, set $\text{Im}\{G(j\omega)H(j\omega)\} = 0$ after rationalizing the denominator.
5. **Evaluate the magnitude** $\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ at this frequency.
6. **Compute GM:** Take the reciprocal of the magnitude (linear) or the negative log-magnitude (dB).
7. **Interpret:** If $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} > 0$, the system is stable; if $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} < 0$, the system is unstable.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1–2:** The Nyquist stability criterion is fundamentally a frequency-domain technique; we must analyze the *steady-state sinusoidal response*.
- **Step 3–4:** A phase shift of −180° means the feedback signal becomes *negatively reinforcing* (instead of negatively corrective), turning the closed loop into a *positive feedback* condition.
- **Step 5–6:** When the magnitude at this critical phase equals 1 (i.e., 0 dB), the loop gain precisely cancels the inherent phase reversal, and the system is on the boundary of instability. GM quantifies the *safety factor*.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180°$ | Condition to find $\omega_{pc}$ | degrees / radians |
| 2 | $\text{Im}\{G(j\omega)H(j\omega)\} = 0$ | Algebraic equivalent (after rationalization) | — |
| 3 | $\text{GM} = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ | Linear Gain Margin | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} = -20 \log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ | Logarithmic Gain Margin | **decibels (dB)** |
| 5 | $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(\text{GM}_{\text{linear}})$ | Conversion formula | dB |
| 6 | $\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)H(j\omega)\} = -\dfrac{1}{K}$ | K-constant locus for conditionally stable systems | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Caution (KTU Exam Typing):** When writing $\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ in answer sheets or markdown tables, **never** use the vertical bar `|`. Always use `\vert` in LaTeX or write the word *magnitude of* explicitly to avoid formatting errors.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Industries:** Tuning PID controllers in chemical reactors — GM between **6 dB and 12 dB** is generally accepted as a robust design target.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control systems demand GM > **8 dB** and Phase Margin > **45°** to handle aerodynamic disturbances and sensor noise.
- **Power Electronics:** SMPS and DC-DC converter compensation networks are sized using GM/PM specifications to prevent subharmonic oscillations.
- **Robotics:** Servo motor position loops use GM analysis to ensure no overshoot-induced instability during rapid acceleration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gain Margin from First Principles (Nyquist Criterion)

Starting from the **Characteristic Equation** of a closed-loop system:

$$
1 + G(s)H(s) = 0
$$

For the system to be marginally stable, there must exist a frequency $\omega = \omega_{pc}$ on the imaginary axis such that $s = j\omega_{pc}$ satisfies the characteristic equation. Substituting:

$$
1 + G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -1 + j0
$$

This complex number has magnitude **1** and phase **−180°** (or equivalently, +180°). In polar form:

$$
\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert \angle \angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = 1 \angle -180°
$$

Equating phase and magnitude:

$$
\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180° \quad \text{and} \quad \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = 1
$$

Now, the **Gain Margin** is defined as the *factor by which the open-loop gain $K$ must be multiplied to reach this marginal condition*. If the current gain is $K$ and the marginal gain is $K_{max}$:

$$
\text{GM} = \frac{K_{max}}{K} = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}
$$

Taking $20 \log_{10}$ on both sides:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} = -20 \log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert\;}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> This is a model KTU 14-mark style problem. Trace each step.

**Problem:** For a unity feedback system with
$$
G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}
$$
find the Gain Margin when $K = 10$.

**Step 1 — Convert to frequency response:** Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$
G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K}{(j\omega)(j\omega+2)(j\omega+5)}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the phase angle** by expressing each factor in polar form:

$$
\angle G(j\omega) = \angle K - \angle(j\omega) - \angle(j\omega+2) - \angle(j\omega+5)
$$

$$
\angle G(j\omega) = 0° - 90° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{5}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the phase condition** $\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$:

$$
-90° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = -180°
$$

$$
\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = 90°
$$

**Step 4 — Use the tangent addition identity** to eliminate the arctangents. For $A + B = 90°$, $\tan(A+B) \to \infty$, which means the denominator of the addition formula is zero:

$$
\tan(A+B) = \dfrac{\tan A + \tan B}{1 - \tan A \tan B}
$$

Setting $1 - \tan A \tan B = 0$:

$$
1 - \left(\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right)\left(\dfrac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = 0
$$

$$
1 - \dfrac{\omega_{pc}^{2}}{10} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega_{pc}^{2} = 10 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the magnitude** at $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10}$:

$$
\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{K}{\omega \cdot \sqrt{\omega^{2}+4} \cdot \sqrt{\omega^{2}+25}}
$$

Substituting $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10}$ and $K = 10$:

$$
\vert G(j\sqrt{10}) \vert = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{10} \cdot \sqrt{10+4} \cdot \sqrt{10+25}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{10} \cdot \sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{35}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{10 \cdot 14 \cdot 35}} = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{4900}} = \dfrac{10}{70} = \dfrac{1}{7}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Gain Margin** in linear and dB scales:

$$
\text{GM} = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\sqrt{10}) \vert} = \dfrac{1}{1/7} = 7
$$

$$
\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(7) \approx 16.9 \text{ dB}
$$

**Interpretation:** $\text{GM} = 7$ means the open-loop gain $K$ can be increased by a factor of **7** (from 10 to 70) before the system becomes marginally stable. The positive 16.9 dB value confirms closed-loop stability.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of GM Values

| $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}}$ | Linear GM | System Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| $> 20$ dB | $> 10$ | Overly conservative, sluggish response |
| $6$ to $20$ dB | $2$ to $10$ | **Good balance** (typical design range) |
| $0$ to $6$ dB | $1$ to $2$ | Marginally stable, sensitive to parameter drift |
| $0$ dB | $1$ | Sustained oscillations (marginal stability) |
| $< 0$ dB | $< 1$ | **Unstable** (Nyquist encircles −1 + j0) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gain Margin on a Bode Magnitude Plot

The following block-level functional architecture illustrates how Gain Margin is geometrically located on a standard Bode magnitude plot.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Bode Magnitude Plot] --> B[Locate Phase Crossover Frequency omega_pc]
    B --> C[Mark the Magnitude Curve Point at omega_pc]
    C --> D[Draw Vertical Line from Curve down to Frequency Axis]
    D --> E[Identify Magnitude Value in dB at omega_pc]
    E --> F[Draw Horizontal Line from Curve to 0 dB Reference Line]
    F --> G[Measure Vertical Distance Between Curve and 0 dB Line]
    G --> H[This Vertical Distance Equals GM in dB]
    H --> I{GM Greater than 0?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Stable System: GM Positive]
    I -->|No| K[Unstable System: GM Negative]
    J --> L[End: Report GM dB Value]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Open-Loop Frequency Response Evaluation

```mermaid
graph LR
    Input[Input Signal Reference] --> SumJunction[Summing Junction]
    SumJunction --> Controller[Controller Gc of s]
    Controller --> Plant[Plant Gp of s]
    Plant --> Output[Process Output Y of s]
    Output --> Feedback[Feedback Sensor H of s]
    Feedback --> SumJunction
    Plant -.-> AnalysisBlock[Open Loop OLTF G of s times H of s]
    AnalysisBlock --> FreqDomain[Substitute s = j omega]
    FreqDomain --> PhaseCalc[Compute Phase Angle]
    PhaseCalc --> PhaseCrossover[Find omega pc where Phase equals -180 deg]
    PhaseCrossover --> MagCalc[Compute Magnitude at omega pc]
    MagCalc --> GMResult[Gain Margin GM = 1 over Magnitude]
```

## 4.3 Stability Decision Flowchart Using Gain Margin

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Obtain Open Loop Transfer Function] --> S1[Substitute s = j omega]
    S1 --> S2[Find omega pc from phase equals -180]
    S2 --> S3[Calculate magnitude at omega pc]
    S3 --> S4[Compute GM dB equals -20 log magnitude]
    S4 --> S5{GM dB greater than 0?}
    S5 -->|Yes Stable| S6[System is Stable, Report Positive GM]
    S5 -->|No Unstable| S7[System is Unstable, Report Negative GM]
    S6 --> S8[Cross check with Phase Margin for Robustness]
    S7 --> S8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the above diagrams are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and free of reserved keywords, ensuring clean rendering across GitHub, VS Code preview, and KTU e-learning portals.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q:** Define Gain Margin. What does a negative gain margin indicate about a control system?

**Model Answer:**
Gain Margin (GM) is the amount of open-loop gain that can be increased before the closed-loop system reaches the verge of instability. It is measured at the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$ where the open-loop phase is −180°.

$$
\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} = -20 \log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert
$$

A **negative gain margin** indicates that the magnitude of the open-loop transfer function at the phase crossover frequency is *greater than 1* (i.e., above the 0 dB line on the Bode plot). This means the Nyquist plot **encircles the −1 + j0 point**, and the closed-loop system is **unstable**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q:** What is phase crossover frequency? How is it related to gain margin?

**Model Answer:**
The **Phase Crossover Frequency (ω_pc)** is the frequency at which the open-loop phase angle crosses −180° (or odd multiples thereof). Mathematically:

$$
\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°
$$

**Relation to Gain Margin:** The Gain Margin is the *reciprocal* of the open-loop magnitude **evaluated specifically at** $\omega_{pc}$. Without $\omega_{pc}$, GM cannot be computed. The phase crossover frequency identifies *when* in the frequency spectrum the system is most vulnerable to instability, and the gain margin quantifies *by how much* the gain can grow at that vulnerable frequency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations (ESE) mandate an *internal choice* in Part B. Two alternatives are provided below; examiners may set either.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for Gain Margin in terms of the open-loop transfer function. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function
$$
G(s) = \dfrac{20}{s(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s)}
$$
compute the **Gain Margin** and the **phase crossover frequency**. Comment on the stability. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

The closed-loop characteristic equation is $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$. Substituting $s = j\omega$ for the marginally stable case:

$$
1 + G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = 0
$$

$$
G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -1 + j0
$$

Converting to polar form, this point has magnitude 1 and angle −180°. Therefore:

$$
\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°
$$

The Gain Margin is defined as the reciprocal of the magnitude of $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ at the phase crossover frequency:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{GM} = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}\;}
$$

In decibels:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(\text{GM}) = -20 \log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert\;}
$$

For stability, the Nyquist plot must *not encircle* −1 + j0, which geometrically means $\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert < 1$, yielding $\text{GM} > 1$ and $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} > 0$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Substitute $s = j\omega$ into the OLTF:

$$
G(j\omega) = \dfrac{20}{j\omega\,(1+0.5j\omega)\,(1+0.2j\omega)}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the phase angle:

$$
\angle G(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega)
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the −180° condition:

$$
-90° - \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega_{pc}) = -180°
$$

$$
\tan^{-1}(0.5\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega_{pc}) = 90°
$$

Using the tangent addition identity, $\tan(90°) \to \infty$, so the denominator must vanish:

$$
1 - (0.5\omega_{pc})(0.2\omega_{pc}) = 0
$$

$$
1 - 0.1\,\omega_{pc}^{2} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega_{pc}^{2} = 10 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the magnitude at $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10}$:

$$
\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{20}{\omega \cdot \sqrt{1+0.25\omega^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{1+0.04\omega^{2}}}
$$

At $\omega = \sqrt{10}$:

$$
\omega = 3.162,\quad 0.25\omega^{2} = 2.5,\quad 0.04\omega^{2} = 0.4
$$

$$
\vert G(j\sqrt{10}) \vert = \dfrac{20}{3.162 \cdot \sqrt{3.5} \cdot \sqrt{1.4}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{3.5} \approx 1.871,\quad \sqrt{1.4} \approx 1.183
$$

$$
\vert G(j\sqrt{10}) \vert \approx \dfrac{20}{3.162 \cdot 1.871 \cdot 1.183} \approx \dfrac{20}{7.0} \approx 2.857
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the Gain Margin:

$$
\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} = -20 \log_{10}(2.857) \approx -20 \times 0.456 = -9.12 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 6:** Stability comment: Since $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} \approx -9.12$ dB (negative), the closed-loop system is **unstable**. The open-loop gain must be *reduced* by approximately 9.12 dB (a factor of about 2.857) to bring the system to the stability boundary.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how Gain Margin is determined from the Bode plot. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A unity feedback control system has $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+4)(s+10)}$. Find the value of $K$ for which the system is marginally stable. What is the corresponding Gain Margin for $K = 50$? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Bode Plot Sketch (7 Marks):**

**Explanation:**
On a Bode magnitude plot (dB vs. log frequency), the Gain Margin is the *vertical distance* between the magnitude curve and the 0 dB axis, **measured at the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$**.

**Sketch Description (textual block diagram):**

```
Magnitude (dB)
   |
   |\
   | \  <-- Slope changes at corner frequencies
20dB|  \
   |   \________
   |    \       \________
0dB|-----\------------------ ω (log scale)
   |      \ω_pc
   |       \   ↑
   |        \  | GM (dB) = vertical distance
   |         \ |
   |          \|
   |           \_______  <-- magnitude curve continues
   |
   +-------------------|--> Frequency (log)
```

**Procedure:**
1. Plot the asymptotic magnitude curve using corner frequencies.
2. Identify $\omega_{pc}$ from the phase plot (where phase = −180°).
3. At $\omega_{pc}$, read the magnitude in dB.
4. $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} = -(\text{magnitude in dB at } \omega_{pc})$.
5. If the curve lies **below** 0 dB at $\omega_{pc}$ → GM positive → stable.
6. If the curve lies **above** 0 dB at $\omega_{pc}$ → GM negative → unstable.

**Part (b) — Marginal Stability (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** The phase condition for OLTF $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K}{j\omega(j\omega+4)(j\omega+10)}$:

$$
\angle G(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{4}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{10}\right) = -180°
$$

$$
\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{4}\right) + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{10}\right) = 90°
$$

**Step 2:** Using the tangent addition identity:

$$
1 - \left(\dfrac{\omega}{4}\right)\left(\dfrac{\omega}{10}\right) = 0
$$

$$
1 - \dfrac{\omega^{2}}{40} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{40} = 2\sqrt{10} \approx 6.32 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Magnitude condition at marginal stability: $\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = 1$:

$$
\dfrac{K}{\omega_{pc}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^{2}+16}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^{2}+100}} = 1
$$

At $\omega_{pc}^{2} = 40$:

$$
\sqrt{40+16} = \sqrt{56},\quad \sqrt{40+100} = \sqrt{140}
$$

$$
K = \omega_{pc}\sqrt{56}\sqrt{140} = \sqrt{40} \cdot \sqrt{56 \times 140}
$$

$$
56 \times 140 = 7840
$$

$$
K = \sqrt{40 \times 7840} = \sqrt{313600} = 560
$$

**Marginal stability gain:** $K_{max} = 560$.

**Step 4:** For $K = 50$, the Gain Margin is:

$$
\text{GM}_{\text{linear}} = \dfrac{K_{max}}{K} = \dfrac{560}{50} = 11.2
$$

$$
\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(11.2) \approx 20.98 \text{ dB}
$$

**Result:** $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} \approx 20.98$ dB (positive) → system is **stable** with a comfortable gain margin.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing GM with Phase Margin:** GM is measured at $\omega_{pc}$ (where phase = −180°), while Phase Margin is measured at $\omega_{gc}$ (where magnitude = 0 dB). Mixing these up is the #1 reason for a zero in Part B.
> 2. **Sign Error in the dB Formula:** Forgetting the negative sign in $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} = -20\log_{10}\vert \cdot \vert$. The *negative* is essential — it makes the value come out positive for stable systems.
> 3. **Units Confusion:** Always quote GM in **dB** when the question says "in dB" or "in decibels"; quote it as a pure number otherwise. Mixing units loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Missing Stability Comment:** Even in purely numerical problems, KTU examiners allocate 1 mark for the *qualitative stability interpretation* (positive GM → stable; negative GM → unstable).
> 5. **Skipping the Phase Crossover Calculation:** Many students jump directly to the magnitude without explicitly solving for $\omega_{pc}$. Always show the phase equation and its solution for full marks.
> 6. **Pipe Symbol in Answer Sheets:** As emphasized in Section 2, never use bare `|` to denote magnitude; the LaTeX `\vert` or the phrase "magnitude of" is the KTU-preferred convention.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Gain Margin** is a *frequency-domain relative stability* measure, evaluated at $\omega_{pc}$.
- ✅ Defined as the **reciprocal** of the open-loop magnitude at the phase crossover frequency: $\text{GM} = 1 / \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$.
- ✅ In decibels: $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} = -20 \log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$.
- ✅ **Phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$:** frequency at which the OLTF phase is −180°.
- ✅ **Stability criterion:** $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} > 0$ dB (linear GM > 1) → stable; $\text{GM}_{\text{dB}} < 0$ dB → unstable.
- ✅ **Design rule of thumb:** Target GM between **6 dB and 12 dB** for robust industrial controllers.
- ✅ **Bode plot interpretation:** GM is the *vertical distance* from the magnitude curve to the 0 dB axis at $\omega_{pc}$.
- ✅ **Nyquist plot interpretation:** GM is the *reciprocal* of the distance from the origin to the curve's intersection with the negative real axis.
- ✅ **Marginal stability:** GM = 1 (linear) = 0 dB → system oscillates at $\omega_{pc}$.
- ✅ **Complementary metric:** Always evaluate Gain Margin *together* with Phase Margin for a complete stability picture.
- ✅ **Master the tangent addition identity** — it is the algebraic key to solving $\omega_{pc}$ for almost every KTU problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Margin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET525 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Phase Margin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Phase Margin (PM or $\gamma$)** is a fundamental measure of **relative stability** in a closed-loop linear control system, evaluated in the **frequency domain** using Bode plots or Nyquist plots. It is defined as the amount of additional phase lag (measured in **degrees** or **radians**) that can be introduced into the open-loop transfer function $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ at the **gain crossover frequency** ($\omega_{gc}$) before the closed-loop system becomes **marginally stable** (i.e., before the system reaches the verge of instability).

Mathematically, the phase margin is expressed as:

$$\gamma = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc}) H(j\omega_{gc})$$

where:
- $\gamma$ = Phase Margin (in degrees)
- $\omega_{gc}$ = **Gain Crossover Frequency** — the frequency at which the magnitude of the open-loop transfer function equals unity (0 dB), i.e., $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$
- $\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ = Phase angle of the open-loop transfer function at $\omega_{gc}$ (negative value, since most physical systems are phase-lagging)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Phase Margin, along with Gain Margin, is a *direct indicator* of the damping, overshoot, and transient response behavior of a closed-loop system. KTU examiners frequently test the **calculation of PM from Bode plots** and the **interpretation of PM for stability** in ESE (End Semester Examination) questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tightrope Walker" Intuition

Imagine a **tightrope walker** crossing a high wire:
- The tightrope is the **stability boundary** of the system.
- The walker carries a long balancing pole — this represents the **phase margin**.
- The further the pole tilts *before* the walker falls, the larger the **phase margin**, and the more stable the walk.
- If the pole tilts just a little and the walker falls — that is a **small phase margin** (oscillatory/unstable behavior).
- If the pole is rigid and the walker stands perfectly upright — that is a **huge phase margin** (overly damped/sluggish system).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Explanation:** Think of Phase Margin as the **safety buffer** in the phase domain. Just as a temperature controller keeps the actual temperature slightly *below* a critical setpoint to avoid overheating, the phase margin keeps the system's phase *above* the critical $-180^\circ$ instability line by a comfortable margin at the gain crossover frequency.

## 1.3 Standard Stability Metrics Associated with Phase Margin

| Metric | Typical Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase Margin ($\gamma$)** | **$\gamma > 0$** | Stable system |
| | $\gamma = 0$ | Marginally stable (sustained oscillations) |
| | $\gamma < 0$ | Unstable system |
| **Recommended $\gamma$ for design** | **$30^\circ \le \gamma \le 60^\circ$** | Good transient response, low overshoot |
| **Ideal $\gamma$** | **$45^\circ$ to $60^\circ$** | Balanced speed and stability |
| **Critical value** | $\gamma \le 0$ | System oscillates or becomes unstable |

> [!WARNING]
> A very large phase margin (e.g., $\gamma > 70^\circ$) makes the system **over-damped and sluggish**, while a very small phase margin (e.g., $\gamma < 30^\circ$) makes the system **under-damped and oscillatory**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Phase Margin Exists

In a negative-feedback closed-loop system, the characteristic equation is:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad G(s)H(s) = -1$$

For stability, the **Nyquist contour** must not encircle the critical point $-1 + j0$. The proximity of the $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ plot to this point determines stability margins.

The **gain crossover frequency** ($\omega_{gc}$) is where:

$$\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert_{dB} = 0 \text{ dB} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$$

At this exact frequency, the system has unity loop gain. The phase of the loop transfer function at $\omega_{gc}$ directly determines how close the system is to the $-1$ point. If the phase at $\omega_{gc}$ is exactly $-180^\circ$, then $G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) = -1$ — the system sits **on** the instability point.

**Phase Margin quantifies the angular distance** from the phase at $\omega_{gc}$ to the critical $-180^\circ$ line.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow for Calculating Phase Margin

1. **Obtain the open-loop transfer function** $G(s)H(s)$.
2. **Substitute** $s = j\omega$ to get $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$.
3. **Calculate the magnitude** $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert$ and the **phase** $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ as functions of $\omega$.
4. **Find the gain crossover frequency** $\omega_{gc}$ by solving:
$$\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$$
5. **Evaluate the phase** at this frequency: $\phi_{gc} = \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$.
6. **Compute Phase Margin** using:
$$\gamma = 180^\circ + \phi_{gc}$$
7. **Interpret** the result using the standard stability chart.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently tested in KTU):** Phase Margin is measured at the **gain crossover frequency** ($\omega_{gc}$, where $\vert GH \vert = 1$), whereas **Gain Margin** is measured at the **phase crossover frequency** ($\omega_{pc}$, where $\angle GH = -180^\circ$). Do NOT confuse these two frequencies in exams.

## 2.3 Relationship Between Phase Margin and Damping Ratio

For a standard second-order system approximation, the phase margin is empirically related to the **damping ratio** $\zeta$ and the **closed-loop overshoot** $M_p$:

$$\zeta \approx \frac{\gamma (\text{in degrees})}{100}$$

A more accurate empirical formula is:

$$M_p (\%) \approx \left(0.6 \cdot \gamma - 5\right) \quad \text{for } 30^\circ \le \gamma \le 70^\circ$$

This means: every **$10^\circ$ increase in $\gamma$** reduces the overshoot by approximately **6%**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Phase Margin Definition** | $\gamma = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ | Degrees |
| 2 | **Gain Crossover Condition** | $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Phase Margin in Radians** | $\gamma_{rad} = \pi + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ | Radians |
| 4 | **Magnitude in dB at $\omega_{gc}$** | $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 0$ | dB |
| 5 | **PM & Damping Ratio (approx.)** | $\zeta \approx \gamma/100$ | Approximate, valid for typical second-order systems |
| 6 | **PM for Minimum-Phase Stable System** | $\gamma > 0$ | Stability requirement |
| 7 | **Overshoot vs. PM (empirical)** | $M_p \approx 0.6\gamma - 5$ | Percent overshoot |
| 8 | **Stability via Bode Plot** | $\gamma > 0$ AND $GM > 0$ dB | Both conditions must hold |
| 9 | **Bandwidth Relation** | $\omega_{BW} \approx \omega_{gc}$ (first-order approx.) | Rad/s |
| 10 | **Critical PM (Marginal)** | $\gamma = 0^\circ$ | Sustained oscillation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In the design of **flight control systems**, **robotic manipulators**, and **process controllers** (e.g., PID tuning via Ziegler-Nichols), the phase margin is the single most important frequency-domain metric. Aerospace engineers demand $\gamma \ge 45^\circ$ for passenger aircraft autopilot systems. In industrial PID tuning, achieving $\gamma \approx 30^\circ$ to $60^\circ$ ensures robustness against plant parameter variations.

## 2.5 Physical Interpretation of Phase Margin in Time Domain

- **$\gamma \approx 90^\circ$:** System behaves like a **first-order** lag — heavily damped, no overshoot, slow response.
- **$\gamma \approx 60^\circ$:** System has **moderate damping** — small overshoot ($\sim 10$–$15\%$), good response speed.
- **$\gamma \approx 45^\circ$:** System has **acceptable damping** — overshoot around $20$–$25\%$, often used as a design target.
- **$\gamma \approx 30^\circ$:** System is **lightly damped** — significant overshoot ($30$–$40\%$), possible ringing.
- **$\gamma \approx 0^\circ$:** System is **marginally stable** — sustained sinusoidal oscillations.
- **$\gamma < 0^\circ$:** System is **unstable** — oscillations grow unboundedly.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Phase Margin from Open-Loop Transfer Function

### Problem Setup

Let the open-loop transfer function of a unity feedback system be:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

where $K$ is the open-loop gain and $\tau$ is the time constant of the system.

### Step 1: Substitute $s = j\omega$

To convert the transfer function into the frequency domain, we substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K}{j\omega(j\omega\tau + 1)} = \frac{K}{j\omega \cdot (1 + j\omega\tau)}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Magnitude

The magnitude of $G(j\omega)$ is the product of magnitudes of numerator and denominator:

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\vert K \vert}{\vert j\omega \vert \cdot \vert 1 + j\omega\tau \vert}$$

Using the identity $\vert j\omega \vert = \omega$ and $\vert 1 + j\omega\tau \vert = \sqrt{1 + \omega^2\tau^2}$:

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K}{\omega \sqrt{1 + \omega^2\tau^2}}$$

### Step 3: Convert to Decibel Form

For Bode plot analysis, we use the magnitude in dB:

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{K}{\omega \sqrt{1 + \omega^2\tau^2}}\right)$$

This can be expanded as:

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} K - 20 \log_{10} \omega - 20 \log_{10} \sqrt{1 + \omega^2\tau^2}$$

### Step 4: Find the Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$

At $\omega_{gc}$, the magnitude equals unity (or 0 dB). Setting $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$:

$$\frac{K}{\omega_{gc} \sqrt{1 + \omega_{gc}^2 \tau^2}} = 1$$

Squaring both sides:

$$\frac{K^2}{\omega_{gc}^2 (1 + \omega_{gc}^2 \tau^2)} = 1$$

Rearranging:

$$K^2 = \omega_{gc}^2 (1 + \omega_{gc}^2 \tau^2)$$

Expanding the right-hand side:

$$K^2 = \omega_{gc}^2 + \omega_{gc}^4 \tau^2$$

Rearranging into a standard quartic form in $\omega_{gc}$:

$$\tau^2 \omega_{gc}^4 + \omega_{gc}^2 - K^2 = 0$$

### Step 5: Solve for $\omega_{gc}$ Using the Quadratic Formula

Let $x = \omega_{gc}^2$. The equation becomes:

$$\tau^2 x^2 + x - K^2 = 0$$

This is a quadratic in $x$. Applying the quadratic formula:

$$x = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1 + 4\tau^2 K^2}}{2\tau^2}$$

Since $x = \omega_{gc}^2 \ge 0$, we take the positive root:

$$x = \omega_{gc}^2 = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4\tau^2 K^2}}{2\tau^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_{gc} = \sqrt{\frac{\sqrt{1 + 4\tau^2 K^2} - 1}{2\tau^2}}$$

### Step 6: Compute the Phase of $G(j\omega)$

The phase of $G(j\omega)$ is the sum of phases of numerator and denominator:

$$\angle G(j\omega) = \angle K - \angle j\omega - \angle (1 + j\omega\tau)$$

For $K > 0$ (positive real number, phase = 0):

$$\angle G(j\omega) = 0^\circ - 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$$

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$$

### Step 7: Evaluate the Phase at $\omega_{gc}$

Substituting $\omega = \omega_{gc}$:

$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc}) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{gc} \tau)$$

### Step 8: Calculate the Phase Margin

Using the definition:

$$\gamma = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

For unity feedback, $H(s) = 1$:

$$\gamma = 180^\circ + \left(-90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{gc}\tau)\right)$$

$$\gamma = 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{gc}\tau)$$

### Step 9: Final Compact Expression

$$\boxed{\gamma = 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{gc} \tau)}$$

This is the **closed-form phase margin expression** for the given second-order system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The first term ($90^\circ$) arises from the integrator $1/s$ in the loop. The second term $\tan^{-1}(\omega_{gc}\tau)$ is the *additional* phase lag contributed by the first-order lag $(1 + \tau s)^{-1}$ at the gain crossover frequency. As $K$ increases, $\omega_{gc}$ increases, $\tan^{-1}(\omega_{gc}\tau)$ increases, and $\gamma$ **decreases** — illustrating the well-known trade-off: **higher gain reduces stability margin**.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

### Given:
$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$$

### Step 1: Substitute $s = j\omega$

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega + 1)(j\omega + 5)}$$

### Step 2: Magnitude Expression

$$\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{10}{\omega \sqrt{1 + \omega^2}\sqrt{25 + \omega^2}}$$

### Step 3: Set Magnitude = 1 at $\omega_{gc}$

$$10 = \omega_{gc} \sqrt{1 + \omega_{gc}^2} \sqrt{25 + \omega_{gc}^2}$$

### Step 4: Solve Iteratively / By Approximation

For a first estimate, we neglect the higher-order terms. At low-to-mid frequencies, $\omega^2$ terms dominate. A common engineering approximation is to use the asymptotic Bode plot, where the corner frequencies are at $\omega = 1$ and $\omega = 5$ rad/s.

For the asymptotic magnitude plot:
- From $\omega = 0$ to $\omega = 1$: slope $= -20$ dB/decade
- From $\omega = 1$ to $\omega = 5$: slope $= -40$ dB/decade
- Above $\omega = 5$: slope $= -60$ dB/decade

The DC gain in dB is $20 \log_{10}(10/\omega)$ at low frequencies. For the asymptotic Bode plot, we find $\omega_{gc}$ in the slope $= -40$ dB/decade region (between $\omega = 1$ and $\omega = 5$):

$$0 = 20\log_{10}(10) - 20\log_{10}(\omega_{gc}) - 20\log_{10}(\omega_{gc})$$

This is in the $-40$ dB/dec region:

$$20\log_{10}(10) - 40\log_{10}(\omega_{gc}/1) = 0$$

$$20 = 40 \log_{10}(\omega_{gc})$$

$$\log_{10}(\omega_{gc}) = 0.5$$

$$\omega_{gc} = 10^{0.5} = 3.162 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Phase at $\omega_{gc}$

$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{gc}/1) - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{gc}/5)$$

Substituting $\omega_{gc} = 3.162$:

$$\angle G(j3.162)H(j3.162) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(3.162) - \tan^{-1}(3.162/5)$$

$$\angle G(j3.162)H(j3.162) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(3.162) - \tan^{-1}(0.6324)$$

Computing the arctangents:

$$\tan^{-1}(3.162) = 72.45^\circ$$

$$\tan^{-1}(0.6324) = 32.31^\circ$$

Therefore:

$$\angle G(j3.162)H(j3.162) = -90^\circ - 72.45^\circ - 32.31^\circ = -194.76^\circ$$

### Step 6: Compute the Phase Margin

$$\gamma = 180^\circ + (-194.76^\circ) = -14.76^\circ$$

### Step 7: Interpretation

Since $\gamma < 0$, the closed-loop system is **UNSTABLE** for $K = 10$.

To stabilize, we must **reduce the gain** $K$ or apply **lead compensation**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget that the asymptotic Bode plot gives only an **approximate** $\omega_{gc}$. For final answer accuracy in KTU exams, always use the **exact magnitude equation** and solve numerically (Newton-Raphson or trial-and-error) if required. The asymptotic method is acceptable only when the problem explicitly allows it.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Phase Margin Computation Flow

The following block-level architecture illustrates the **computational sequence** to extract the phase margin from a given open-loop transfer function:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Open Loop TF G(s)H(s)] --> B[Substitute s = j omega]
    B --> C[Compute Magnitude: M omega = absolute value of GH]
    C --> D[Set M omega gc = 1]
    D --> E[Solve for Gain Crossover Frequency omega gc]
    E --> F[Compute Phase: phi omega = angle GH]
    F --> G[Evaluate Phase at omega gc]
    G --> H[Apply PM Formula: gamma = 180 + phi gc]
    H --> I[OUTPUT: Phase Margin gamma in degrees]
    I --> J{Is gamma greater than zero?}
    J -->|YES| K[System is STABLE]
    J -->|NO| L[System is UNSTABLE]
    K --> M[Check Design Criteria: 30 to 60 degrees]
    L --> N[Apply Compensation: Lead Lag PID]
```

## 4.2 Bode Plot Schematic — Visualizing Phase Margin

The following diagram is a **block-level functional representation** of how Phase Margin is geometrically identified on a Bode magnitude-phase plot:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAG[ "MAGNITUDE PLOT (dB vs log omega)" ]
        M1[High Frequency: Roll off at -20 dB/dec or steeper]
        M2[0 dB Line: Critical Reference Axis]
        M3[Gain Crossover Point: omega gc where curve crosses 0 dB]
        M4[Low Frequency: Flat gain at 20 log K]
    end
    subgraph PHS[ "PHASE PLOT (degrees vs log omega)" ]
        P1[Phase starts at -90 degrees for Type 1 system]
        P2[Phase decreases with frequency]
        P3[Critical Line: -180 degrees]
        P4[Phase at omega gc: phi gc]
    end
    P4 --> PM[Phase Margin: vertical distance from phi gc to -180 degree line at omega gc]
    M3 --> GC[Gain Crossover Frequency omega gc is the LINKING point]
    GC --> PM
```

## 4.3 Nyquist Plot Interpretation of Phase Margin

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Start at origin of Nyquist plot] --> N2[Plot G j omega H j omega as omega goes from 0 to infinity]
    N2 --> N3[Find point where curve crosses unit circle: this is omega gc]
    N3 --> N4[Draw radius vector from origin to this point]
    N4 --> N5[Measure angle between negative real axis and this vector]
    N5 --> N6[This angle IS the phase margin gamma]
    N6 --> N7[If radius falls on negative real axis: gamma = 0 marginally stable]
    N7 --> N8[If radius falls on positive real axis: gamma is positive STABLE]
    N8 --> N9[If radius falls beyond negative real axis: gamma is negative UNSTABLE]
```

## 4.4 Phase Margin vs. Time-Domain Performance Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Phase Margin gamma] --> B1[gamma = 90 deg]
    A --> B2[gamma = 60 deg]
    A --> B3[gamma = 45 deg]
    A --> B4[gamma = 30 deg]
    A --> B5[gamma = 0 deg]
    A --> B6[gamma less than 0 deg]
    B1 --> C1[Overdamped: No overshoot, very slow]
    B2 --> C2[Well damped: ~10 percent overshoot]
    B3 --> C3[Moderately damped: ~22 percent overshoot]
    B4 --> C4[Underdamped: ~37 percent overshoot, ringing]
    B5 --> C5[Pure oscillation: Marginally stable]
    B6 --> C6[Unstable: Growing oscillations]
```
